question: What have been the changes in European men's formal garb from the high middle ages to that of the Renaissance
answer: [['14th century', 'breeches made of linen and a shirt also made of linen as undergarments. Hose or chausses made out of wool were used to cover the legs, and often had leather soles, so that they did not have to be worn with shoes. A doublet was a buttoned jacket that was generally of hip length. A robe, tunic, or kirtle was usually worn over the shirt or doublet, over this, a man might also wear an over-kirtle, cloak, or a hood. the chaperon made a transformation from being a utilitarian hood with a small cape to becoming a complicated and fashionable hat worn by the wealthy in town settings.'], ['11th century', 'The tunic, a long fabric panel, folded over with a neck-hole cut into the fold, and sleeves attached. The tunic was usually belted, with either a leather or strong fabric belt. Trousers were tailored either loose or tight (or not worn at all if the weather was warm). Leggings were strips of cloth wound round the leg, held in place by long laces. A cloak or mantle, fastened by a brooch, completed the outfit. For headgear, the cappa or chaperon was a one-piece hood and cape over the shoulders was worn for cold weather, and the Roman straw hat for summer fieldwork. Shoes, were mostly the simple turnshoe of a cowhide sole and softer leather upper, which were sewn together, and then turned inside out.'], ['12th century', 'Underclothes consisted of a linen tunic or shirt with long, tight sleeve. Tailored cloth leggings called chausses or hose, made as separate garments for each leg, the hose became longer and more fitting and reached above the knees. Over the undertunic and hose, men wore an outer tunic that reached to the knees or ankles, and that was fastened at the waist with a belt. Fitted bliauts, of wool or, increasingly, silk, had sleeves that were cut wide at the wrist and gored skirts. Men wore bliauts open to the waist front and back or at the side seams. The newly introduced doublet and the quilted and padded jupe or gipon were short, fitted garments for the upper body, worn under the tunic. The sleeveless surcoat or cyclas was introduced as protective covering for armour. Rectangular and circular cloaks were worn over the tunic, fastened on the right shoulder or at the center front. As headgear, the chaperon in the form of hood and attached shoulder-length cape was worn, as well as the fitted linen coif tied under the chin. Small round or slightly conical caps with rolled brims were worn, and straw hats were worn for outdoor work in summer.'], ['13th century', 'Men wore a tunic, cote, or cotte with a surcoat over a linen shirt. One of these surcoats was the cyclas, which began as a rectangular piece of cloth with a hole in it for the head, where the sides were sewn together to make a long, sleeveless tunic. When sleeves and sometimes a hood were added, the cyclas became a garnache (a cap-sleeved surcoat, usually shown with hood of matching color) or a gardecorps (a long, generous-sleeved traveling robe, somewhat resembling a modern academic robe). A mantle was worn as a formal wrap. Men also wore hose, shoes, and headdress. Men wore long braies or leggings with legs of varying length. Hose could be worn over this, attached to the drawstring or belt at the waist. Hats included a round cap with a slight brim, the beret, the coif (a little tight white hood with strings that tied under the chin), the straw hat, and a hood that came round the neck and over the shoulders. Shoes were slightly pointed, and embroidered for royalty and higher clergy.'], ['15th century', 'The basic costume of men in this period consisted of a shirt, doublet, and hose, with some sort of overrobe. Linen shirts were worn next to the skin. Over the shirt was worn a doublet, very tight-fitting doublets, belted or tailored to be tight at the waist, giving in effect a short skirt below, were fashionable, with the sleeves being full and even puffy. Men wore short braies or breeches, a loose undergarment, usually made of linen, which was held up by a belt. Hose or chausses made out of wool were used to cover the legs, and were generally brightly colored. As doublets became shorter, hose reached to the waist rather than the hips, and were sewn together into a single garment with a pouch or flap to cover the front opening; this evolved into the codpiece. The Houppelande, or cioppa, was the overgarment of the wealthy, it was a robe with fullness falling from the shoulders in organ-pipe pleats and very full sleeves often reaching to the floor. A sideless tunic or tabard, called a giornea or journade was popular. For headgear, the hood remained popular, although it was frequently worn around the neck as a cowl or twisted into a chaperon. Hats began to compete with the chaperon. Hats of vaious styles: tall-crowned with small brims or no brims at all, hats with brims turned up on one side for variations of the coif, or low-crowned with wider brims pulled to a point in front. For footwear the duckbill shoe and long-toed poulaines were popular.']]

question: For every OECD country, what is the manner by which its Supreme Court judges are appointed
answer: [['Australia', 'Appointed by Governor-General following nomination by Prime Minister, and advice from Attorney-General'], ['Belgium', 'nominated by the High Council of Justice of Belgium and appointed by the Belgian federal government.'], ['Chile', 'appointed by the President and ratified by a two-thirds majority of the Senate.'], ['Denmark', 'appointed by the Minister of Justice on the recommendation of the Independent Board of Judges.'], ['Estonia', 'Riigikogu'], ['Germany', 'elected by the Bundestag (the German parliament) and the Bundesrat (a legislative body that represents the sixteen state governments on the federal level).'], ['Hungary', 'The Hungarian Constitution declares that members of the Constitutional Court shall be elected by Parliament.'], ['Iceland', 'selected by the Qualifications Committee and nominated by the Minister of Justice for presidential confirmation.'], ['Ireland', 'Appointed by the President, acting on the binding advice of the Government'], ['Israel', 'Presidential appointment upon nomination by the Judicial Selection Committee'], ['Japan', 'Selected by the Cabinet of Japan'], ['South Korea', "Chief Justice of the Supreme Court with the consent of the 'Council of Supreme Court Justices'"], ['Latvia', 'The Saeima approves judges of the Supreme Court.'], ['Netherlands', 'Selected by the House of Representatives on advice of the Supreme Court and appointed by royal decree'], ['New Zealand', 'Appointed by Attorney General'], ['Norway', "The King of Norway has sole authority to appoint judges to the country’s Supreme Court and other senior courts. He does so on the advice of the country's Judicial Appointments Board, a body whose members are also appointed by the King.["], ['Poland', 'The President of Poland appoints Supreme Court judges for an indefinite period.'], ['Portugal', '60 judges appointed on selection, 4 military judges appointed on proposal by the Military Staff'], ['Slovakia', 'The judges of the Supreme Court are appointed by the President of the Slovak Republic after being seen as qualified enough by the Judicial Council of the Slovak Republic.'], ['Spain', 'nominated by the General Council of the Judiciary and appointed by the Monarch'], ['Sweden', 'Government-appointed with parliamentary notification'], ['Switzerland', 'proposed by the Judicial Committee and elected by the United Federal Assembly (National Council and Council of States) for a term of office of six years.'], ['United Kingdom', 'An independent selection commission is to be formed when vacancies arise. This is to be composed of the President of the Supreme Court (the chair), another senior UK judge (not a Supreme Court Justice), and a member of the Judicial Appointments Commission of England and Wales, the Judicial Appointments Board for Scotland and the Northern Ireland Judicial Appointments Commission.'], ['United States', 'the president, with the advice and consent of the Senate, appoints a new justice.'], ['Austria', 'The responsibility for appointing justices is vested in the president of Austria, but the president can and usually does delegate this power to the minister of justice.'], ['Canada', 'appointed on the advice of the prime minister'], ['Colombia', 'elected by the same institution in list conformed by the Superior Council of the Judiciary'], ['Costa Rica', 'selected for the CSJ for 8-year terms by the Legislative Assembly'], ['Finland', 'appointed by the President of the Republic'], ['Mexico', 'They are ratified through affirmation by the Senate from a list proposed by the President of the Republic. The ministers chosen will select from among themselves who shall be the President of the Court to serve a four-year period; any given minister may serve out more than one term as president, but may not do so consecutively.'], ['Slovenia', 'elected by the National Assembly'], ['Lithuania', 'appointed by the Seimas from the candidates presented by the President (three judges), Chairman of Seimas (three judges) and the chairman of the Supreme Court (three judges)'], ['France', 'approved by a special panel, the High Council of the Judiciary.']]

In the kingdom of France, what were the royal houses and how did they come to replace the previous royal house
answer: [['House of Bourbon (1589–1792)', 'Margaret of Valois, the last legitimate Valois descendant, named her son with Henry of House Bourbon, as her heir'], ['Robertian dynasty', 'The Robertian family figured prominently amongst the Carolingian nobility and married into the royal family.'], ['Bosonid dynasty', 'married into the Carolingian dynasty and produced kings and an emperor of the Frankish Empire'], ['House of Valois-Angoulême', 'Francis I succeeded his first cousin once removed and father-in-law Louis XII, who died without a legitimate son'], ['Carolingian dynasty (Carolingiens)', 'The family consolidated its power in the 8th century, making the offices of mayor of the palace and dux et princeps Francorum hereditary.'], ['House of Bourbon', 'Henry III of France died without a male heir, and Under the Salic law, the head of the House of Bourbon, as the senior representative of the senior-surviving branch of the Capetian dynasty, became King of France as Henry IV.'], ['Merovingian dynasty (Mérovingiens)', 'The Merovingian family united the Franks and northern Gallo-Romans under their rule and then conquered Gaul.'], ['House of Poitiers (Maison de Poitiers)', 'Ramnulf II, claimed the title of King of Aquitaine, and his illegitimate son Ebalus fathered the line of dukes of Aquitaine that would rule continuously from 927 to 1204'], ['Bosonids (Bosonides)', 'Bosonid nobles married into the Carolingian dynasty and later produced kings and a Frankish emperor.'], ['House of Capet', 'Hugh Capet secured the throne of France by election'], ['House of Valois', 'Capetian Dynasty kings died without male heirs; Philip, Count of Valois had been chosen as Regent beforehand, and became King in turn.'], ['House of Valois-Orléans', 'Charles VII died childless, allowing his second cousin and brother-in-law Louis of Orléans to succeed to the throne.'], ['House of Bourbon-Vendôme', "Antoine became King of Navarre by marriage. Upon the extinction of all male lines of the House of Valois in 1589, Antoine's son Henry became King Henry IV of France"], ['House of Orléans', 'The July Revolution of 1830 deposed King Charles X and replaced him with the more liberal Louis Philippe I'], ['House of Lancaster', "During the Hundred Years' War, Plantagenet King Henry VI of England, of the House of Lancaster, enjoyed de jure control of the French throne following the Treaty of Troyes"], ['House of Bonaparte', 'Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France and made this family members Kings of conquered European territories.'], ['Carolingian dynasty', 'The family consolidated power behind the Merovingian kings until Pepin the Short dethroned the ruling monarch to become king himself.'], ['House of Bourbon-Orléans', 'The July Revolution of 1830 deposed King Charles X and replaced him with the more liberal Louis Philippe I'], ['Robertian dynasty (Robertiens)', 'The Robertian family married into the royal Carolingian family and produced kings.']]

answer: ['Plurality voting refers to electoral systems in which the candidate in an electoral district who poll more than any other (that is, receive a plurality) are elected. In SMP/FPTP the leading candidate, whether or not they have a majority of votes, is elected.', 'Party-list proportional representation (list-PR) is a subset of proportional representation electoral systems in which multiple candidates are elected (e.g., elections to parliament) through their position on an electoral list.
